enrichment.short_description: Canny is a customer feedback and product roadmap platform that helps SaaS teams collect feature requests, prioritize them with AI, and publish public changelogs.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Canny helps product teams collect feedback, prioritize features with AI Autopilot, and share public roadmaps and changelogs. Free plan available.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Canny is a customer feedback and product management platform used by software companies to collect feature requests, prioritize them, and communicate what they are building. Founded in 2015 by Sarah Hum and Andrew Rasmussen, former Meta employees, Canny is headquartered in San Francisco and has operated as a bootstrapped, profitable business rather than a venture-funded startup. The core workflow centers on three connected surfaces: feedback boards where users and internal teams submit and upvote ideas, one or more roadmaps that show what is planned, in progress, and shipped, and a changelog that announces completed work back to the people who requested it. Canny's AI feature, called Autopilot, is included on every plan including the free tier; it automatically groups duplicate or related feedback posts, summarizes long comment threads, drafts replies, and surfaces themes across large volumes of submissions so product teams do not have to manually triage every entry. Feedback can be captured directly inside Canny's own hosted boards or pulled in from third-party channels such as Intercom, Zendesk, Slack, and a Chrome extension, letting support and sales teams log a customer request without leaving their existing tools. On the delivery side, Canny integrates with issue trackers and project management tools including Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana, GitHub, and Azure DevOps, so prioritized items can be pushed to an engineering backlog without manual re-entry. Pricing follows a freemium, tracked-user-based model. The Free plan supports up to 25 tracked users and five manager seats with unlimited posts and boards. The Pro plan, priced at $79 per month when billed yearly (roughly 20 percent cheaper than monthly billing), raises the tracked-user ceiling and adds custom domains, content translation, project management integrations, user segmentation, automation rules, and custom admin roles, and is described by Canny as its most popular tier. The Business plan is a custom-quoted tier for larger organizations that adds single sign-on, CRM integrations, and enterprise-scale support, and requires contacting Canny's sales team for pricing. Canny also offers reduced-rate plans for qualified non-profits and high-volume consumer products. The product is aimed at product managers, founders, and customer success teams at SaaS companies who need a single, public or private system of record for what customers are asking for, rather than tracking requests in spreadsheets or support tickets. Typical users include B2B software vendors publishing a public roadmap to build trust with customers, and internal product teams using Canny privately to prioritize a backlog. Because Canny is bootstrapped and has been operating profitably for close to a decade, it is often cited in the SaaS community as an example of a durable, founder-led feedback tool. Canny's own feedback portal is itself an example of the product, since the company uses Canny internally to manage its own roadmap and changelog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Canny is feedback management software that gives product teams a single, structured place to collect user requests, decide what to build, and tell customers when it ships. It replaces the common pattern of feedback scattered across support tickets, spreadsheets, and sales notes with public or private voting boards, a visual roadmap, and a built-in changelog.

The company was founded in 2015 by Sarah Hum and Andrew Rasmussen, who met working as a designer and engineer at Meta, and is headquartered in San Francisco. Canny has grown as a bootstrapped, profitable company rather than a venture-backed one, which it frequently cites as evidence of long-term product stability.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Feedback is captured through customizable boards where users and teammates submit and upvote ideas, and can also flow in automatically from Intercom, Zendesk, Slack, and a Chrome extension so support teams can log requests without switching tools.

Canny's AI layer, Autopilot, is bundled into every plan, including Free. It deduplicates similar submissions, summarizes long comment threads, and drafts suggested replies, reducing the manual triage work of running a feedback inbox.

Prioritized feedback can be pushed directly into engineering workflows through native integrations with Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana, GitHub, and Azure DevOps, keeping the roadmap and the backlog in sync without manual re-entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Canny uses a freemium, tracked-user-based pricing model with three published tiers. Free covers up to 25 tracked users with unlimited posts, boards, and Autopilot AI. Pro, at $79/month billed yearly, adds custom domains, translations, PM tool integrations, segmentation, and automation rules for larger or more structured teams.

The top-tier Business plan is not publicly priced and requires a sales conversation; it adds SSO, CRM integrations, and support for very high tracked-user volumes. Canny offers a roughly 20% discount for annual billing and has separate reduced pricing for qualified non-profits.

features.description: A built-in changelog widget to announce shipped features back to the customers who requested them.

features.description: Pulls in requests from Intercom, Zendesk, Slack, and a Chrome extension.

features.title: Project Management Integrations
features.description: Pushes prioritized items to Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana, GitHub, and Azure DevOps.

features.description: Pro-tier controls for targeting feedback prompts to specific user segments and restricting admin permissions.

pros: Generous free plan with core AI features included
Bootstrapped, profitable company with a long operating track record
Deep integrations with common support and engineering tools
Simple, purpose-built interface compared to general-purpose project trackers
cons: Business-tier pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Tracked-user pricing can grow quickly for products with large user bases
No native support ticketing of its own — relies on integrations for that
Portal customization is more limited on Free and Pro than on Business

faqs.question: Is Canny free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Canny has a permanent Free plan for up to 25 tracked users with unlimited posts, boards, and access to Autopilot AI; paid plans start at $79/month for higher usage tiers.

faqs.question: Who founded Canny?
faqs.answer: Canny was founded in 2015 by Sarah Hum and Andrew Rasmussen, who previously worked together as a designer and engineer at Meta.

faqs.question: What is Canny's Autopilot AI?
faqs.answer: Autopilot is Canny's built-in AI layer that deduplicates similar feedback, summarizes comment threads, and drafts replies. It's available on every plan, including Free.

faqs.question: Does Canny integrate with Jira and Linear?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Pro and Business plans include native integrations with Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana, GitHub, and Azure DevOps for pushing prioritized feedback into engineering workflows.

faqs.question: Can I use a custom domain for my Canny feedback portal?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom domains are available starting on paid plans, letting companies host their feedback board and changelog under their own domain.

use_cases.description: Product managers consolidate requests from support tickets, sales calls, and in-app prompts into one prioritized backlog.

use_cases.title: Customer-Facing Changelogs
use_cases.description: Teams announce shipped features back to the specific customers who requested them, closing the feedback loop.

features: PM tool integrations (Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana)

enrichment.short_description: Canva is a web-based design platform offering drag-and-drop tools, AI-powered Magic Studio features, and hundreds of thousands of templates for social media, presentations, and print.
enrichment.seo_title: Canva: AI Design Platform, Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Canva is a free-to-start design platform with AI Magic Studio tools, templates, and team plans. See Canva's pricing, key features, and plan comparison.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Canva is a web-based graphic design and visual content platform founded in Perth, Australia, and officially launched in August 2013 by Melanie Perkins, Cliff Obrecht, and Cameron Adams. Perkins conceived the idea while teaching university students to use complex desktop design software and wanted a simpler, browser-based alternative; after being turned down by more than 100 investors, she and Obrecht eventually built the company into one of the most highly valued private software companies in the world, headquartered in Sydney, Australia, with additional offices including Manila, and a workforce that has grown past 4,000 employees. Canva's core product is a drag-and-drop design editor that runs in the browser and as native apps for iOS, Android, Mac, and Windows, giving non-designers a way to produce social media graphics, presentations, documents, videos, websites, and printed marketing materials from a single library of templates. The Free plan includes access to more than 250,000 templates, 5GB of cloud storage, and Canva's basic editing, template, and stock-asset library at no cost, which is a major reason Canva has scaled to tens of millions of monthly active users across consumer, education, and business segments. Paid tiers unlock more capability rather than gating the core editor. Canva Pro costs $15 per month or $120 per year for a single user and adds premium templates and stock content, 1TB of storage, unlimited Brand Kits, a background remover, and Canva's Magic Studio suite of generative AI tools — including Magic Write for text generation, Magic Design for automated layout suggestions, Magic Eraser, Magic Expand, and AI-assisted video and image editing. The Business plan (formerly sold as Canva for Teams) costs $20 per user per month or $200 per user per year and adds shared brand controls, approval workflows, and team-wide asset libraries for small and mid-sized organizations; Canva has been migrating existing customers off the older flat-rate Teams plan toward this per-seat Business pricing. An Enterprise plan is available for large organizations that need single sign-on, SCIM user provisioning, audit logs, and a dedicated customer success manager, priced on a custom quote and generally requiring a minimum of roughly 150 seats. Canva also sells a separate AI Pass add-on for Pro and Business subscribers, priced around $100 per person per month, that substantially raises the usage limits on its AI features beyond what is bundled into the base subscription. Beyond the design editor, Canva has expanded into an adjacent productivity suite: Canva Docs for word processing, Canva Whiteboards for visual collaboration, Canva Presentations, and Canva Sheets, alongside a print-and-merchandise fulfillment service and a developer platform (Canva Apps SDK and Canva Connect API) that powers an apps marketplace with more than 300 third-party integrations for tools like Slack, HubSpot, Dropbox, and Asana. Canva for Education and Canva for Nonprofits provide the Pro feature set for free to verified teachers, students, and registered nonprofits. Typical users range from individual creators and small business owners who need professional-looking graphics without design training, to marketing teams at larger companies that use Canva's Brand Kit and approval workflows to keep visual output on-brand across many contributors. Because of its low barrier to entry, broad template library, and integrated AI tools, Canva is commonly positioned as an alternative to a combination of Adobe Creative Cloud, PowerPoint, and stock-photo subscriptions for teams that do not need professional-grade pixel-level design control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Canva was founded in 2013 by Melanie Perkins, Cliff Obrecht, and Cameron Adams, after Perkins spent years teaching students how to use traditional desktop design software and grew frustrated with its complexity. The company is headquartered in Sydney, Australia, employs over 4,000 people, and has become one of the most widely used design tools in the world, used by individuals, marketing teams, educators, and nonprofits.

Canva's editor runs in a browser and as native apps for iOS, Android, Mac, and Windows. It is built around a large template library covering social media graphics, presentations, documents, videos, print materials, and websites, with a Free plan that includes more than 250,000 templates and 5GB of storage at no cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Magic Studio is Canva's suite of generative AI tools, including Magic Write for text generation, Magic Design for automated layout suggestions, Magic Eraser and Magic Expand for photo editing, and AI-assisted video tools. These are unlocked starting on the Pro plan.

For teams, Canva Business adds Brand Kits, approval workflows, and shared team libraries to keep visual output consistent across many contributors. Canva has also expanded beyond design into an adjacent productivity suite — Canva Docs, Whiteboards, Presentations, and Sheets — plus a print-and-merchandise fulfillment service.

A developer platform (Canva Apps SDK and Canva Connect API) powers an apps marketplace with 300+ third-party integrations, including Slack, HubSpot, Dropbox, and Asana, letting teams connect Canva to their existing workflow tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Canva uses a four-tier freemium model. Free includes the core editor and template library. Pro is $15/month or $120/year per user and adds premium content, 1TB storage, and Magic Studio AI tools. Business is $20/user/month or $200/user/year and adds brand and approval controls for teams; it has replaced the older flat-rate Canva Teams plan for new customers.

Enterprise is custom-quoted, generally requires around 150+ seats, and adds SSO, SCIM provisioning, audit logs, and a dedicated customer success manager. Canva also sells an AI Pass add-on (around $100/person/month) for Pro and Business users who need significantly higher AI usage limits. Canva for Education and Canva for Nonprofits provide the Pro feature set free to verified teachers, students, and registered nonprofits.

features.title: Drag-and-Drop Editor

features.title: Magic Studio AI Tools
features.description: Generative AI suite including Magic Write, Magic Design, Background Remover, and Magic Eraser.

features.description: 250,000+ templates spanning social media, presentations, documents, video, and print.

features.title: Brand Kit & Approval Workflows
features.description: Team-wide brand asset libraries and content approval steps on the Business plan.

features.title: Canva Docs, Whiteboards & Presentations
features.description: Adjacent productivity tools for documents, visual collaboration, and slide decks.

features.title: Apps Marketplace & Connect API
features.description: 300+ third-party integrations plus a developer platform for building custom Canva apps.

features.title: Print & Merchandise Fulfillment
features.description: Order printed products (cards, apparel, signage) directly from a Canva design.

pros: Free plan is genuinely usable with a large template and asset library
Magic Studio AI tools speed up common design and copywriting tasks
Cross-platform apps for web, iOS, Android, Mac, and Windows
Free Pro-equivalent access for verified teachers, students, and nonprofits
Broad integration ecosystem via the apps marketplace
cons: Less precise, pixel-level control than professional tools like Photoshop or Illustrator
Enterprise plan requires a roughly 150-seat minimum and custom pricing
AI Pass add-on is a significant extra cost on top of Pro or Business
Most features require an internet connection, limiting offline design work

faqs.question: Is Canva free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Canva's Free plan is permanently free and includes 250,000+ templates, 5GB of storage, and the core editor.

faqs.question: What does Canva Pro include?
faqs.answer: Canva Pro ($15/month or $120/year) adds 1TB of storage, premium templates and stock content, a background remover, and the Magic Studio AI tools.

faqs.question: What is Magic Studio?
faqs.answer: Magic Studio is Canva's suite of generative AI design tools, including Magic Write, Magic Design, Magic Eraser, and Magic Expand.

faqs.question: Is Canva free for teachers and students?
faqs.answer: Yes, Canva for Education provides the Pro feature set at no cost to verified K-12 teachers and students.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Canva Pro and Business?
faqs.answer: Pro is priced per individual user, while Business ($20/user/month) adds team features like shared Brand Kits, approval workflows, and multi-user administration.

faqs.question: Does Canva have an API for developers?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Canva Connect API and Canva Apps SDK let developers build integrations and apps that appear in Canva's apps marketplace.

enrichment.short_description: Canvas LMS is Instructure's cloud-based learning management system for K-12 and higher-ed institutions, also available as free, self-hosted open-source software.
enrichment.pricing_model: Institutional / custom (also free as self-hosted open source)
enrichment.seo_title: Canvas LMS: Features, Pricing & Open Source Option
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Canvas LMS by Instructure is a cloud learning management system used by 4,000+ schools, with a free, self-hosted open-source version also available.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Canvas is a learning management system (LMS) created by Instructure, a Salt Lake City, Utah-based education technology company founded in 2008 by Brigham Young University graduate students Brian Whitmer and Devlin Daley. Instructure built Canvas as a cloud-native alternative to older, on-premise LMS platforms such as Blackboard, and launched the product in 2011. Canvas is now used by more than 4,000 educational institutions and roughly 28 million students, teachers, and administrators worldwide, spanning K-12 schools, universities, and corporate or professional training programs. A distinguishing feature of Canvas is that it is dual-licensed: Instructure sells and hosts a commercial version of Canvas to institutions under custom, negotiated contracts, while the underlying codebase is also released as free, open-source software under the AGPLv3 license on GitHub, allowing any organization with the technical resources to self-host and run Canvas at no licensing cost. Institutional pricing for the hosted commercial version is not published; third-party estimates commonly cite a per-student annual range, though actual contract terms vary widely by institution size and negotiated terms, and prospective customers must contact Instructure's sales team for a quote. Core Canvas features include a course and content management system where instructors build modules, assignments, quizzes, and discussion boards; a Gradebook that supports customizable grading schemes and weighted categories; and SpeedGrader, a tool that lets instructors view a student's submission, rubric, and grade entry on one screen, annotate PDFs directly, and leave text or video feedback. Canvas also includes Mastery Paths and integration with Instructure's Mastery Connect and Outcomes tools, which let instructors build competency-based learning sequences that branch based on how a student performs on a given assessment. Beyond the core LMS, Instructure offers companion products under the same Canvas umbrella, including Canvas Studio for video-based learning and Canvas Commons for sharing course content between instructors and institutions. Canvas has one of the most extensively documented APIs in the LMS category, with several hundred REST endpoints, and it supports the LTI (Learning Tools Interoperability) standard, which lets institutions connect Canvas to more than 1,000 third-party educational tools, from publisher content to proctoring and plagiarism-detection services. Native mobile apps are available for both students and instructors on iOS and Android, covering assignment submission, discussion participation, grade checking, and mobile SpeedGrader for on-the-go feedback. Instructure previously offered a free hosted account for individual teachers outside of an institutional contract, but took that specific offering offline following a security incident in 2026; the self-hosted open-source version remains available at no cost to anyone willing to run and maintain their own servers. Canvas is best suited for accredited schools, universities, and training organizations that need a scalable, API-extensible LMS with a large third-party integration ecosystem, as opposed to individual educators or very small training providers looking for an out-of-the-box, no-setup tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Canvas is a learning management system built by Instructure, founded in 2008 by BYU graduate students Brian Whitmer and Devlin Daley and headquartered in Salt Lake City, Utah. Canvas launched in 2011 as a cloud-native alternative to older on-premise LMS platforms and is now used by more than 4,000 institutions and roughly 28 million students, teachers, and administrators globally.

Canvas is unusual among major LMS platforms in that it is dual-licensed: Instructure sells a fully hosted, supported commercial version to institutions, while the same core codebase is released as free, open-source software under the AGPLv3 license, so technically capable organizations can self-host it without paying a licensing fee.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's core is course and content management — modules, assignments, quizzes, and discussion boards — paired with a customizable Gradebook and SpeedGrader, which lets instructors view a submission, rubric, and grade field on one screen, annotate PDFs, and leave text or video feedback.

Mastery Paths and Instructure's Outcomes and Mastery Connect tools support competency-based learning that branches based on assessment performance. Canvas Studio adds video-based learning, and Canvas Commons lets instructors and institutions share course content with one another.

Canvas has one of the more extensively documented APIs in the LMS category and supports the LTI interoperability standard, connecting to more than 1,000 third-party educational tools. Native mobile apps for students and teachers cover assignment submission, discussions, grading, and mobile SpeedGrader.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Institutional pricing for the Instructure-hosted commercial version is not publicly published and is negotiated per contract based on factors like enrollment size; prospective customers need to request a quote from Instructure's sales team.

Because the core Canvas codebase is open source under AGPLv3, organizations with their own technical infrastructure can self-host Canvas at no licensing cost, though they take on the work of installation, maintenance, and support themselves rather than relying on Instructure's managed hosting and support.

features.title: Gradebook
features.description: Customizable grading schemes and weighted categories.

features.title: Mastery Paths & Outcomes
features.description: Competency-based learning paths that branch based on assessment performance.

features.title: Canvas Studio
features.description: Video-based learning tools integrated into the course experience.

features.title: Canvas Commons
features.description: Shared content library for exchanging course materials between instructors and institutions.

features.title: Open API & LTI Integrations
features.description: Well-documented REST API and LTI support connecting to 1,000+ third-party tools.

pros: Deep, well-documented API and LTI ecosystem for integrations
Free, self-hostable open-source version available under AGPLv3
Strong grading and feedback tools (SpeedGrader) valued by instructors
Widely adopted, so training resources and course content are broadly transferable between institutions
cons: Commercial hosted pricing is not published and requires a sales negotiation
Self-hosting the open-source version requires in-house technical infrastructure and maintenance
Feature set and update cadence can differ between the hosted commercial product and the open-source release
Can feel like more platform than necessary for very small training programs

faqs.question: Is Canvas LMS free?
faqs.answer: The source code is free and open-source under AGPLv3 for organizations that self-host it. The fully hosted, supported commercial version sold by Instructure requires a paid institutional contract.

faqs.question: Who owns Canvas LMS?
faqs.answer: Canvas is built by Instructure, founded in 2008 by Brian Whitmer and Devlin Daley and headquartered in Salt Lake City, Utah.

faqs.question: What is SpeedGrader?
faqs.answer: SpeedGrader is Canvas's grading tool that shows a student's submission, rubric, and grade field on one screen, with PDF annotation and video feedback.

faqs.question: Does Canvas have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Instructure offers native iOS and Android apps for both students and teachers.

faqs.question: Can Canvas integrate with other education tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, via its open REST API and support for the LTI standard, connecting to more than 1,000 third-party educational tools.

features: Full Canvas codebase under AGPLv3 license
Fully hosted and maintained by Instructure
Access to Canvas Studio and Commons

enrichment.short_description: Capacities is an object-based note-taking and personal knowledge management app that organizes information as linked objects instead of files and folders.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Capacities is a note-taking app that organizes ideas as connected objects. See its features, AI assistant, and Free, Pro, and Believer pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Capacities is a personal knowledge management and note-taking application built by Capacities Labs GmbH, a German company founded in 2020 by Steffen Bleher and Michael von Hohnhorst and based in Sankt Wendel, Germany. The product differentiates itself from conventional note apps such as Notion or Obsidian by organizing information around typed objects rather than freeform pages or files. Instead of creating a generic document, a Capacities user creates an object of a defined type — for example a Person, a Book, a Meeting, or a Daily Note — and each object type can carry its own structured properties, such as dates, ratings, or links to other objects, in addition to free-form text content. Objects can be linked to one another, and Capacities renders those links as an interactive graph view, letting users navigate their knowledge base by relationship rather than folder hierarchy. Core features include Daily Notes for quick capture, custom object types and templates, properties and relations for structuring data, tagging and collections for grouping related objects, and an AI assistant, available on paid plans, that can answer questions about a user's own notes, summarize content, and help draft new entries. The app is offline-first: content is stored locally and synced across devices once a connection is available, and users can export their entire knowledge base to Markdown, HTML, or JSON, which the company positions as a way to avoid vendor lock-in. Capacities is available as native desktop apps for Mac, Windows, and Linux, as a web app usable in any browser, and, since mobile apps launched in July 2024, as native iOS and Android apps, with tablet-optimized versions for iPad and Android tablets added shortly after. Pricing follows a freemium model. The Free plan has no cap on the number of notes or objects a user can create and includes 5GB of storage for embedded media, making it usable as a primary note-taking tool without paying. The Pro plan costs $9.99 per month when billed annually, or $11.99 billed monthly, and removes the storage cap while adding access to the AI assistant, saved queries, calendar integration, and priority support. A Believer plan, priced at $12.49 per month annually or $14.99 monthly, includes the same feature set as Pro but is positioned by the company as a way for users to directly support ongoing development of the product. Capacities is aimed at people who think in terms of interconnected concepts rather than linear documents — researchers, writers, and knowledge workers building a structured personal knowledge base — and it competes most directly with Notion, Obsidian, Roam Research, and Tana in the personal knowledge management category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Capacities is a note-taking and personal knowledge management app built by Capacities Labs GmbH, founded in 2020 by Steffen Bleher and Michael von Hohnhorst and based in Sankt Wendel, Germany. Rather than storing information as generic pages or files, Capacities organizes content around typed objects — a Person, a Book, a Meeting, a Daily Note — each with its own structured properties.

Objects can be linked together and explored through an interactive graph view, giving users a way to navigate a knowledge base by relationship instead of a folder tree, which the company positions as a middle ground between the flexibility of networked notes and the structure of a database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include Daily Notes for quick capture, custom object types and templates, properties and relations for structuring data, and tagging and collections for grouping related objects. An AI assistant, available on paid plans, can answer questions about a user's own notes, summarize content, and help draft new entries.

The app is offline-first, syncing across devices once a connection is available, and supports export to Markdown, HTML, or JSON to avoid vendor lock-in. Capacities runs as native apps on Mac, Windows, and Linux, as a web app, and, since July 2024, as native iOS and Android apps with tablet-optimized versions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Capacities offers a Free plan with no cap on notes or objects and 5GB of media storage, making it usable as a primary note app without paying. Pro costs $9.99/month billed annually ($11.99 monthly) and adds unlimited storage, the AI assistant, saved queries, and calendar integration.
A Believer plan, at $12.49/month annually ($14.99 monthly), includes the same features as Pro but is framed by the company as a way for users to directly support ongoing development.

features.title: Object-Based Notes
features.description: Content is organized as typed objects (Person, Book, Meeting, etc.) instead of freeform files.

features.title: Properties & Relations
features.description: Each object type carries structured properties and can be linked to other objects.

features.title: Graph View
features.description: Interactive visualization of how objects and notes connect to one another.

features.title: Daily Notes
features.description: A dedicated space for quick daily capture of tasks, meetings, and ideas.

features.title: AI Assistant
features.description: Paid-plan AI that answers questions about your own notes, summarizes content, and helps draft entries.

features.title: Offline-First Sync & Export
features.description: Local-first storage with cross-device sync, plus export to Markdown, HTML, or JSON.

pros: Usable free tier with unlimited notes and objects
Structured object model gives more built-in organization than plain markdown notes
True cross-platform coverage including native mobile apps
No lock-in thanks to open export formats
cons: Smaller plugin/integration ecosystem than more established tools like Notion or Obsidian
AI assistant is gated behind a paid plan
Learning the object-type model takes longer than starting a blank page in a simpler notes app
Small team size relative to larger competitors may affect roadmap pace

faqs.question: Is Capacities free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan has no limit on notes or objects and includes 5GB of storage for media.

faqs.question: What makes Capacities different from Notion?
faqs.answer: Capacities organizes information as typed objects with structured properties and relations, rather than freeform pages, and renders those connections as an interactive graph.

faqs.question: Does Capacities work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's offline-first — content is stored locally and syncs across devices once a connection is available.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app for Capacities?
faqs.answer: Yes, native iOS and Android apps launched in July 2024, along with tablet-optimized versions for iPad and Android tablets.

faqs.question: Can I export my data out of Capacities?
faqs.answer: Yes, your knowledge base can be exported to Markdown, HTML, or JSON.

use_cases.title: Personal Knowledge Management
use_cases.description: Researchers and writers organize notes, sources, and ideas as linked objects rather than a flat file tree.

use_cases.title: Daily Journaling & Task Capture
use_cases.description: Professionals use Daily Notes to log meetings, tasks, and reflections in a structured way.

enrichment.short_description: Capchase is a fintech platform that gives B2B SaaS companies non-dilutive financing by advancing future subscription revenue and flexible payment options for buyers.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, USA (with offices in Madrid and Barcelona)
enrichment.seo_title: Capchase: Revenue Financing & B2B Payments for SaaS
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Capchase offers non-dilutive revenue financing (Capchase Grow) and flexible B2B payment options (Capchase Pay) for software companies.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Capchase is a financial technology company that provides revenue-based financing and B2B payment infrastructure for software companies. Founded in 2020, Capchase is headquartered in New York with additional offices in Madrid and Barcelona, and has funded more than 4,000 companies with over $2.5 billion in total financing according to the company's own published figures. The company's flagship product, Capchase Grow, is a non-dilutive financing tool that lets SaaS companies borrow against their own future annual recurring revenue (ARR) instead of raising equity or taking on traditional venture debt. Rather than requiring a pitch deck or business plan, Capchase asks applicants to connect their banking, accounting, and billing systems through secure data integrations; its underwriting algorithms then assess the quality and durability of a company's recurring contracts and typically return a financing offer within 24 to 48 hours. Through Capchase Grow, eligible companies can access an advance of roughly 20 to 70 percent of their ARR, with individual transaction sizes ranging from about $25,000 to $10 million and repayment terms generally spanning three to twelve months, though some financing arrangements extend longer. Pricing on Grow is structured as a flat discount fee, commonly cited in the 10 to 12 percent range over the term of the advance, split since 2023 into a separate Platform Fee, roughly 1 to 4 percent depending on financing terms, which covers access to the Capchase Grow platform, and a Financing Fee that covers the cost of capital itself; companies generally need a minimum ARR floor, commonly cited around $150,000, and a minimum cash runway to qualify. Capchase's second core product, Capchase Pay, is a B2B buy-now-pay-later style tool aimed at the sales side of the transaction: it lets a software vendor's customers pay for an annual or multi-year contract in installments while the vendor still receives the full contract value upfront. Vendor customers can be qualified in about a minute using automated data checks, and the flat financing fee on a Capchase Pay transaction can be paid by the vendor, the buyer, or split between the two, with no separate platform, implementation, or integration fees and setup times commonly cited as under 24 hours. Because pricing on both products is negotiated per company and per deal based on underwriting output, Capchase does not publish a fixed-rate pricing page in the way a typical SaaS subscription product would; prospective customers apply through the website and receive individualized terms. Capchase is aimed primarily at venture-backed and growth-stage B2B SaaS and hardware companies that want to smooth out cash flow from annual contracts, extend runway between funding rounds, or offer flexible payment terms to their own customers without absorbing the credit risk themselves, positioning it against alternatives such as traditional venture debt, other revenue-based financing providers, and in-house installment billing built directly into a company's own billing stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Capchase is a fintech company founded in 2020 and headquartered in New York, with additional offices in Madrid and Barcelona. It builds financing and payment products specifically for B2B software companies, and reports having funded more than 4,000 companies with over $2.5 billion in total financing.

The company's underwriting is automated: applicants connect their banking, accounting, and billing systems, and Capchase's algorithms assess the quality of their recurring revenue to return a financing offer, typically within 24 to 48 hours, without a pitch deck or business plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Capchase Grow is the core revenue-based financing product, advancing roughly 20 to 70 percent of a company's ARR against future recurring revenue, with transaction sizes from about $25,000 to $10 million and repayment terms generally spanning three to twelve months.

Capchase Pay addresses the buyer side of a deal: it lets a software vendor's customer pay for an annual or multi-year contract in installments while the vendor still collects the full contract value upfront, with the financing fee payable by the vendor, buyer, or split between them.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Capchase does not publish fixed pricing tiers. Capchase Grow charges a flat discount fee, commonly cited around 10 to 12 percent, split since 2023 into a Platform Fee (roughly 1 to 4 percent) and a separate Financing Fee, and generally requires a minimum ARR floor around $150,000.

Capchase Pay charges a flat financing fee per deal that can be paid by the vendor, the buyer, or split between them, with no separate platform, implementation, or integration fees. Exact terms for both products are determined per company through Capchase's underwriting process.

features.title: Capchase Grow
features.description: Non-dilutive financing that advances a portion of a company's future ARR.

features.title: Capchase Pay
features.description: B2B buy-now-pay-later style financing that lets buyers pay in installments while vendors get paid upfront.

features.title: Automated Underwriting
features.description: Connects banking, accounting, and billing data to return financing offers within 24-48 hours.

features.title: Non-Dilutive Capital
features.description: Financing that doesn't require giving up equity or board seats.

features.title: Flexible Repayment Terms
features.description: Repayment terms generally ranging from 3 to 12 months, depending on the product and deal.

features.title: Split Financing Fees
features.description: On Capchase Pay, the financing fee can be paid by the vendor, the buyer, or split between them.

pros: No equity dilution or board seats required, unlike venture capital
Fast underwriting turnaround, typically 24-48 hours, versus traditional bank loans
Flexible fee-sharing on Capchase Pay between vendor and buyer
Purpose-built for recurring-revenue software businesses rather than generic business loans
cons: Pricing isn't public — companies must apply and go through underwriting to learn their actual cost of capital
Requires minimum ARR and runway thresholds, so it isn't accessible to pre-revenue or very early-stage startups
Flat discount fees on short-term financing can equate to a high effective annual rate compared with traditional debt
Requires connecting banking, accounting, and billing data for underwriting

faqs.question: What is Capchase Grow?
faqs.answer: Capchase Grow is a revenue-based financing product that advances a portion of a SaaS company's future annual recurring revenue in exchange for a flat fee.

faqs.question: What is Capchase Pay?
faqs.answer: Capchase Pay is a B2B payment product that lets a vendor's customers pay for annual or multi-year contracts in installments, while the vendor is paid the full contract value upfront.

faqs.question: Is Capchase financing dilutive?
faqs.answer: No, Capchase's financing products are non-dilutive — companies don't give up equity or board seats.

faqs.question: How fast can a company get funded through Capchase?
faqs.answer: Underwriting decisions are typically returned within 24 to 48 hours after a company connects its banking, accounting, and billing data.

faqs.question: Who is eligible for Capchase financing?
faqs.answer: Primarily B2B SaaS and hardware companies with recurring revenue meeting Capchase's minimum ARR and cash runway requirements.

use_cases.title: Extending Cash Runway Between Funding Rounds
use_cases.description: Growth-stage SaaS companies smooth out cash flow from annual contracts without raising a new equity round.

use_cases.title: Flexible Payment Terms for Customers
use_cases.description: Software vendors offer buyers installment payments on annual contracts while still getting paid upfront themselves.

enrichment.short_description: CapRover is a free, self-hosted platform-as-a-service that turns any Docker-enabled server into a one-click app deployment platform with automatic SSL.
enrichment.seo_title: CapRover: Free Self-Hosted PaaS for Docker Apps
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CapRover is a free, self-hosted PaaS that automates Docker and Nginx to deploy apps, databases, and free SSL certificates on your own server.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CapRover is a free, self-hosted platform-as-a-service (PaaS) that lets developers turn any Docker-enabled server, from a cloud VM on AWS, DigitalOcean, or Hetzner to a Raspberry Pi, into a one-click application deployment platform similar to Heroku. The project was created by Kasra Bigdeli, an engineering leader, who built the first version, originally named CaptainDuckDuck, in October 2017 to reduce his own hosting costs after relying on Heroku for side projects; the project was later renamed CapRover. It has since become one of the more widely used self-hosted PaaS tools among independent developers and small teams, with its official Docker image downloaded more than 150 million times from Docker Hub and its GitHub repository (caprover/caprover) attracting over 15,000 stars. Under the hood, CapRover automates Docker container orchestration, Nginx reverse-proxy configuration, and Let's Encrypt SSL certificate issuance and renewal, removing most of the manual server administration that would otherwise be required to run multiple web applications behind HTTPS on a single machine. Deployments can be triggered from the CapRover CLI, a web-based dashboard, a Git push with an auto-generated webhook, a Dockerfile, or a prebuilt image pulled from Docker Hub or a private registry. CapRover also supports Docker Swarm clustering for teams that need to run apps across more than one node, and it ships with a library of one-click apps that can install common software such as WordPress, MySQL, MariaDB, MongoDB, PostgreSQL, and other popular open-source tools with a single click rather than a manual server setup. Because there is no vendor-hosted service or subscription involved, CapRover has no recurring product fee: the only ongoing cost is whatever server the user chooses to run it on. One nuance worth noting is licensing: CapRover was originally released under the Apache License 2.0, but in July 2023 the project moved to a modified version of that license that places some redistribution restrictions on certain paid or enterprise-oriented features, while the core deployment platform used by most self-hosters remains free to run and its source remains publicly viewable on GitHub. CapRover is best suited for individual developers, small startups, and hobbyists who want Heroku-style deployment convenience without paying Heroku-style hosting fees, and who are comfortable managing their own server instead of using a fully managed cloud platform. It is commonly compared to other self-hosted deployment tools such as Coolify, Dokploy, and Portainer, and to managed alternatives like Render, Railway, and Northflank for teams that would rather not administer their own server at all.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CapRover is a free, self-hosted platform-as-a-service that turns any Docker-enabled server into a one-click app deployment platform. It was created by Kasra Bigdeli, who released the first version, originally called CaptainDuckDuck, in October 2017 to reduce his own hosting costs after relying on Heroku for side projects.

The project has since become widely used among independent developers and small teams: its Docker image has been downloaded more than 150 million times from Docker Hub, and its GitHub repository has attracted over 15,000 stars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: CapRover automates Docker container orchestration, Nginx reverse-proxy configuration, and Let's Encrypt SSL certificate issuance and renewal, removing most of the manual server administration otherwise needed to run multiple HTTPS-secured apps on one machine.

Deployments can be triggered via the CapRover CLI, a web dashboard, a Git push with an auto-generated webhook, a Dockerfile, or a prebuilt image from Docker Hub or a private registry. Docker Swarm clustering is supported for multi-node setups, and a one-click app marketplace can install common software like WordPress and popular databases without manual server setup.

Licensing has evolved: CapRover began under the Apache License 2.0, but in July 2023 moved to a modified version of that license that restricts redistribution of certain paid or enterprise-oriented features, while the core self-hosted platform remains free to run and its source stays publicly viewable on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: There is no CapRover subscription or licensing fee for the core self-hosted platform — it is free to download and run. The only ongoing cost is whatever server infrastructure the user chooses to run it on, which can range from a low-cost VPS to a Raspberry Pi.

features.title: One-Click App Deployment
features.description: Deploy apps via CLI, web dashboard, Git webhook, Dockerfile, or prebuilt image.

features.title: Docker + Nginx Automation
features.description: Automates container orchestration and reverse-proxy configuration behind the scenes.

features.title: Free Automatic SSL
features.description: Automatic Let's Encrypt certificate issuance and renewal for every app and domain.

features.title: Docker Swarm Clustering
features.description: Built-in support for running apps across multiple nodes.

features.title: CLI and Web Dashboard
features.description: Command-line tooling for automation plus a browser-based management UI.

features.title: Git Webhook Deploys
features.description: Auto-generated webhooks for pushing deployments straight from a Git repository.

features.title: One-Click App Marketplace
features.description: Install common software like WordPress and popular databases with a single click.

pros: Completely free to run — the only cost is your own server
Automates SSL, reverse proxying, and container orchestration that would otherwise require manual setup
Works on virtually any Docker-capable machine, including low-cost VPS providers and Raspberry Pi
Large, active community with a track record dating back to 2017
cons: Requires the user to provision, secure, and maintain their own server — there is no managed hosting option from the project itself
No official enterprise support contract or SLA in the way a managed PaaS vendor would offer
Licensing changed in 2023 to a modified Apache License that restricts redistribution of certain paid features
Less polished UI/UX than fully managed commercial PaaS competitors

faqs.question: Is CapRover free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core CapRover platform is free to download and run; the only cost is the server you deploy it on.

faqs.question: What is CapRover built on?
faqs.answer: CapRover automates Docker, Nginx, and Let's Encrypt, with optional Docker Swarm clustering for multi-node setups.

faqs.question: Who created CapRover?
faqs.answer: Kasra Bigdeli created CapRover, originally releasing it as CaptainDuckDuck in October 2017.

faqs.question: Is CapRover open source?
faqs.answer: Its source is publicly available on GitHub. Since July 2023 it has used a modified Apache License that restricts redistribution of certain paid features, rather than a pure OSI-approved open-source license.

faqs.question: Can CapRover run across multiple servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, CapRover supports Docker Swarm clustering for running apps across more than one node.

features: Unlimited apps on your own server

enrichment.short_description: Capsule CRM is a UK-based customer relationship management platform for small and mid-sized businesses, with sales pipelines, project boards, and AI tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Capsule CRM: Pricing, Features & Free Plan
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Capsule CRM is a UK-built CRM for small and mid-sized businesses with sales pipelines, AI enrichment, and a free plan for up to 2 users.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Capsule CRM is a customer relationship management platform built by Zestia Ltd., a company founded in 2009 and headquartered in Manchester, United Kingdom. Capsule has operated for more than 15 years as an independent CRM vendor focused primarily on small and mid-sized businesses rather than large enterprises, positioning itself as a simpler, less configuration-heavy alternative to CRM platforms such as Salesforce or HubSpot. The core product centers on contact and organization management, customizable sales pipelines displayed as Kanban-style boards, task and activity tracking, and project boards for managing work after a deal closes. Capsule integrates natively with common productivity and finance tools, including Gmail, Outlook, Microsoft 365, and Google Workspace for email and calendar sync, Mailchimp for email marketing, Stripe, Xero, and QuickBooks for billing and accounting, and Zapier along with a REST API for custom integrations. Starting at its Growth tier, Capsule has added a set of AI features, including an AI Pipeline Generator that suggests pipeline structures, AI business and contact enrichment that fills in company and contact details automatically, AI-generated summaries of records and activity, and an AI Meeting Prep tool, in beta, that compiles relevant context ahead of a scheduled meeting. Capsule's pricing is tiered by user count and feature access, billed primarily on an annual basis with a 14-day free trial on paid plans. A permanent Free plan supports up to 2 users, 250 contacts, 5 custom fields, and a single sales pipeline, which is enough for a very small team or a solo operator to run basic contact management at no cost. The Starter plan, priced at $18 per user per month on annual billing, raises the contact limit to 30,000, and adds email templates, a shared team mailbox, basic reporting, premium integrations such as Xero and Zendesk, and the AI Pipeline Generator. The Growth plan, at $36 per user per month annually, doubles the contact limit to 60,000 and adds multiple sales pipelines, project boards, workflow automations, advanced sales reporting and dashboards, and the AI enrichment and summary features. The Advanced plan, at $60 per user per month annually, raises the contact ceiling further to 120,000 and increases the number of project boards and pipelines a team can run. The Ultimate plan sits at the top of the range, generally cited around $75 per user per month on annual billing, and adds a dedicated account manager and custom onboarding and training for larger SMB teams that want enterprise-style support without moving to a full enterprise CRM. Capsule also sells an add-on marketing product, Transpond, starting around $11 per month, for lead-capture forms and email marketing campaigns that connect back into the core CRM. Capsule is best suited for small businesses, agencies, and sales teams that want a CRM they can set up and use within a day, rather than a highly configurable enterprise platform that typically requires a dedicated administrator or implementation partner.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Capsule CRM is built by Zestia Ltd., founded in 2009 and headquartered in Manchester, United Kingdom. Capsule has operated for more than 15 years as an independent CRM vendor focused on small and mid-sized businesses, positioning itself as a simpler alternative to larger enterprise CRM platforms.
The core product centers on contact and organization management, customizable Kanban-style sales pipelines, task and activity tracking, and project boards for managing post-sale work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Capsule integrates natively with Gmail, Outlook, Microsoft 365, and Google Workspace for email and calendar sync, plus Mailchimp, Stripe, Xero, QuickBooks, and Zapier, alongside a REST API for custom integrations.

Starting at the Growth tier, Capsule includes AI features: an AI Pipeline Generator that suggests pipeline structures, AI business and contact enrichment, AI-generated summaries, and an AI Meeting Prep tool currently in beta.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Capsule offers a permanent Free plan for up to 2 users and 250 contacts. Paid plans are billed per user, mostly on an annual basis with a 14-day free trial: Starter at $18/user/month, Growth at $36/user/month, Advanced at $60/user/month, and Ultimate around $75/user/month, each raising contact limits and unlocking more automation, reporting, and AI features.
A separate marketing add-on, Transpond, is available starting around $11/month for lead-capture forms and email marketing campaigns that connect back into the core CRM.

features.title: Contact & Organization Management
features.description: Central database of contacts and organizations with custom fields.

features.title: Sales Pipelines
features.description: Customizable, Kanban-style pipelines for tracking deals through stages.

features.title: Task & Project Boards
features.description: Post-sale project management boards for tracking delivery work.

features.title: AI Business & Contact Enrichment
features.description: Automatically fills in company and contact details, available from the Growth plan up.

features.title: AI Summaries & Meeting Prep
features.description: AI-generated record summaries and a beta AI meeting-prep tool.

features.title: Email & Calendar Integrations
features.description: Native sync with Gmail, Outlook, Microsoft 365, and Google Workspace.

features.title: REST API & Zapier
features.description: Custom integrations via a documented REST API or Zapier connections.

pros: Simple enough to set up without a dedicated CRM administrator
Usable permanent free plan for very small teams
Native integrations with common email, accounting, and marketing tools
AI enrichment and summary features included starting at a mid-tier price point
cons: Free plan is capped at just 2 users and 250 contacts
Automations, multiple pipelines, and AI features require higher-priced tiers
Less depth in advanced enterprise features than large enterprise CRMs
Marketing functionality (Transpond) is a separate paid add-on rather than built in

faqs.question: Is Capsule CRM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Capsule has a free plan for up to 2 users and 250 contacts; paid plans start at $18/user/month billed annually.

faqs.question: Where is Capsule CRM based?
faqs.answer: Capsule is built by Zestia Ltd., founded in 2009 and headquartered in Manchester, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Does Capsule CRM have AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, from the Growth tier up: an AI Pipeline Generator, business and contact enrichment, AI summaries, and a beta AI Meeting Prep tool.

faqs.question: Does Capsule integrate with Gmail and Outlook?
faqs.answer: Yes, along with Xero, QuickBooks, Stripe, Mailchimp, Zapier, and a REST API for custom integrations.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app for Capsule CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Capsule offers native iOS and Android apps.

enrichment.short_description: Captions is an AI video creation app that generates automatic captions, AI dubbing, and AI avatar videos, letting creators produce studio-style videos from raw footage.
enrichment.seo_title: Captions: AI Video Editor, Captions & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Captions is an AI video app for auto captions, AI dubbing in 30+ languages, and AI avatar videos. Compare Free, Max, and Scale pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Captions is an AI-powered video creation app founded in 2021 by Gaurav Misra and Dwight Churchill, who previously worked together at Snap Inc. before starting the company. Headquartered in New York City, Captions raised funding from investors including Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, and Index Ventures, and in a 2024 Series C round was valued at roughly $500 million. In September 2025, the parent company renamed itself Mirage to reflect a broader strategic focus on building its own proprietary video-generation foundation model and future video products, while the original Captions app and its feature set continue to operate under the Captions name for end users. The product's original core use case is automatic captioning: users upload raw video and Captions generates on-screen captions in more than 100 languages, along with a library of animated caption styles. From there, the app expanded into a fuller AI video production toolkit that includes automatic scene cutting, B-roll suggestions, transition and music placement, and AI-driven audio dubbing into more than 30 languages with lip-sync, allowing a single piece of footage to be localized for multiple markets without re-shooting. More recent additions include Mirage Studio, launched in June 2025, which generates AI avatar-based videos, including a personal AI Twin digital likeness of the creator, from a text prompt or an audio track, and a chat-based AI editor that lets users describe an edit in natural language rather than manipulating a manual timeline. Captions is available as native apps for iOS, Android, desktop, and web. Pricing follows a freemium, credit-based model. The Free plan includes basic manual editing tools such as trimming and transitions, access to the stock media library, and captioning, but does not include generative AI credits. The Max plan, at $24.99 per month with a discounted annual option, is the entry-level paid tier and includes 500 AI credits per month along with access to the AI Edit style library, full AI-assisted video production, digital-twin custom AI actor creation, the chat-based editor, and AI-generated B-roll, music, and sound effects. Above Max, three Scale tiers, Scale 1x at $69.99 per month with 1,400 credits, Scale 2x at $139.99 per month with 2,800 credits, and Scale 4x at $279.99 per month with 5,600 credits, offer proportionally larger monthly AI credit allowances and access to enhanced AI model options for creators and teams producing higher volumes of video. Unused credits roll over month to month up to three times the monthly allowance. An Enterprise plan is available on custom pricing for organizations that need bulk credit discounts, custom seat counts, dedicated account management, and a guarantee that their content will not be used to train Captions' models. The product is aimed primarily at individual content creators, social media marketers, and small video teams who want to produce short-form talking-head or narrated video content quickly, without traditional timeline-based video editing skills or a professional production setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Captions is an AI video creation app founded in 2021 by Gaurav Misra and Dwight Churchill, former colleagues at Snap Inc., and headquartered in New York City. The company raised funding from Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, and Index Ventures, reaching a roughly $500 million valuation in a 2024 Series C round.

In September 2025, the parent company renamed itself Mirage to reflect a strategic focus on building its own proprietary video-generation foundation model, while the consumer-facing app continues to operate under the Captions name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Captions started as an automatic captioning tool, generating on-screen captions in more than 100 languages, and expanded into a fuller AI video toolkit with automatic scene cutting, B-roll suggestions, and AI dubbing with lip-sync into more than 30 languages, letting a single video be localized for multiple markets.

Mirage Studio, launched in June 2025, generates AI avatar videos, including a personal AI Twin digital likeness of the creator, from a text prompt or audio track. A chat-based AI editor lets users describe an edit in natural language instead of manipulating a manual timeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Captions uses a freemium, credit-based pricing model. The Free plan covers basic manual editing and captioning without AI credits. The Max plan, at $24.99/month, includes 500 monthly AI credits and unlocks the full AI production toolkit, digital-twin creation, and the chat-based editor.

Three Scale tiers scale up the monthly credit allowance for higher-volume creators and teams: Scale 1x ($69.99/month, 1,400 credits), Scale 2x ($139.99/month, 2,800 credits), and Scale 4x ($279.99/month, 5,600 credits). An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with bulk credit discounts, dedicated account management, and training-data exclusion.

features.title: Automatic Captions
features.description: Generates on-screen captions in more than 100 languages from raw video.

features.title: AI Dubbing & Lip-Sync
features.description: Dubs voiceovers into 30+ languages with synced lip movement.

features.title: Mirage Studio
features.description: Generates AI avatar-based videos from a text prompt or audio track.

features.title: AI Twin
features.description: Creates a personal AI digital likeness of the creator for generated videos.

features.title: Chat-Based AI Editor
features.description: Describe an edit in natural language instead of using a manual timeline.

features.title: Automatic Scene Cutting & B-Roll
features.description: AI-assisted scene cuts, B-roll suggestions, transitions, and music placement.

features.title: Credit-Based AI Usage
features.description: Monthly AI credit allowances across mobile, desktop, and web apps.

pros: Free plan available for basic manual editing and captioning
Fast localization via AI dubbing into 30+ languages with lip-sync
No manual timeline editing required thanks to the chat-based AI editor
Available across mobile, desktop, and web
cons: Generative AI features require a paid credit-based plan starting at $24.99/month
Higher-volume creators can hit credit limits quickly on the entry Max plan
AI avatar and digital-twin content raises likeness and consent considerations creators must manage themselves
Training-data exclusion and other enterprise safeguards require a custom-priced plan

faqs.question: Is Captions free?
faqs.answer: There's a free plan for basic manual editing and captioning. Generative AI features require the Max plan ($24.99/month) or higher.

faqs.question: What languages does Captions support for dubbing?
faqs.answer: AI dubbing supports 30+ languages, while auto-captions are available in 100+ languages.

faqs.question: What is Mirage Studio?
faqs.answer: Mirage Studio, launched in June 2025, is Captions' AI avatar video generation tool, including a personal AI Twin feature.

faqs.question: Did Captions change its name?
faqs.answer: The parent company rebranded to Mirage in September 2025 to reflect its foundation-model strategy, while the consumer app continues operating as Captions.

faqs.question: How do AI credits work in Captions?
faqs.answer: Each paid plan includes a monthly AI credit allotment, and unused credits roll over up to three times the monthly allowance.

use_cases.title: Multilingual Content Localization
use_cases.description: Marketing teams dub existing video into other languages with lip-sync to reach global audiences.

features: AI B-roll, music, and sound effects
